short_description: An open-source Linux/Unix privilege-escalation enumeration script in the PEASS-ng suite that audits a host for misconfigurations, vulnerabilities, and environmental conditions that could enable local privilege escalation.
overview_notes: linPEAS is part of the PEASS-ng (Privilege Escalation Awesome Scripts – next generation) project maintained on GitHub. It focuses on local, post-compromise auditing of Linux/Unix-like and macOS systems to highlight likely privilege-escalation vectors using color-coded findings and structured sections. PEASS-ng also provides parsers to transform tool output into machine-readable/report-friendly formats. The project is actively updated and distributed via GitHub releases as shell scripts and prebuilt binaries, with documentation cross-referenced to the HackTricks knowledge base maintained by the same author.
what_it_is_notes: A host-based, read-only (by default) enumerator that inspects system configuration, permissions, services, credentials artifacts, containers/virtualization settings, software versions, and kernel/build information to surface misconfigurations and conditions commonly abused to gain elevated privileges. It is not an exploitation framework; it reports potential paths and leaves validation/exploitation to the operator.
how_it_works_notes: - Executed locally on the target host as a POSIX shell script (and also available as compiled binaries). The script runs modular checks covering OS/system info, users/groups/sudoers, file permissions (including SUID/SGID), services/processes/timers/cron, environment and PATH risks, network and storage mounts (NFS, fstab), credentials in predictable locations, software/package versions, kernel/build flags, containerization (Docker/LXC/Kubernetes indicators), and more. Findings are highlighted to aid triage.  - linPEAS is built from modular components via a builder system within PEASS-ng, enabling variants that include ‘full’, ‘default’, and ‘small’ check sets.  - Output can be post-processed by official PEASS-ng parsers to JSON/HTML/PDF for analysis and reporting.  - By design, it avoids writing to disk and avoids credential use; some optional extended checks increase runtime and noisiness.
inputs: An interactive shell or code execution context on a Linux/Unix-like or macOS host
Host environment context (filesystem, process list, network, packages, kernel/build info)
Optional: offline filesystem directory for static analysis of extracted firmware or root filesystems
outputs: Human-readable, color-highlighted console output summarizing potential privilege-escalation vectors
Structured output suitable for parsing using PEASS-ng parsers (JSON/HTML/PDF via separate tooling)
Contextual pointers to knowledge-base content (HackTricks) for further analysis
core_concepts: Misconfiguration discovery (sudo, SUID/SGID, PATH, services, timers/cron)
Kernel and software version risk context
typical_workflow: Obtain low-privileged execution on a Linux/Unix-like host during an engagement.
Run linPEAS to enumerate system- and user-level misconfigurations and conditions associated with privilege escalation.
Review highlighted findings (e.g., risky sudo rules, writable service files, dangerous SUID binaries, exposed credentials, insecure mounts, container escape indicators).
Correlate findings with trusted references (e.g., GTFOBins entries for specific binaries; vendor/CVE notes for kernel or package versions).
Manually validate feasibility, impact, and exploitability of shortlisted vectors within the agreed rules of engagement.
Document confirmed risks and recommended remediations; optionally export/parse output for reporting and tracking.
use_cases: Rapid triage of local privilege-escalation opportunities during penetration tests or red team assessments after initial foothold.
Purple/blue team hardening reviews to identify common misconfigurations and weak controls on Linux fleets.
Control validation for EDR/monitoring by observing detections when enumeration touches sensitive areas.
Baseline checks in lab environments and CTFs to speed up manual analysis.
Offline analysis of extracted firmware or filesystem images to spot hardening gaps before deployment.
limitations: Requires code execution on the host; may be noisy and trigger EDR/AV or monitoring alerts.
Findings are suggestive, not definitive; false positives/negatives can occur depending on platform specifics and environment hardening.
Not an exploitation tool—operators must validate and execute any escalation steps separately and ethically.
Coverage varies by OS/distribution/version; some checks depend on available system utilities and permissions.
Runtime can be significant on large filesystems or when extended checks are enabled; some optional checks may briefly create and remove temporary artifacts.
Operational security considerations apply (tool provenance, on-disk artifacts if not executed purely in-memory, potential network egress when cross-referencing).

related_tools.name: WinPEAS
related_tools.relationship: Windows counterpart within the PEASS-ng suite for local privilege-escalation enumeration on Windows hosts.

related_tools.name: macOS-PEAS (via linPEAS auto-detection)
related_tools.relationship: When executed on macOS, linPEAS switches to a macOS-focused enumeration mode within the same suite.

related_tools.name: GTFOBins
related_tools.relationship: Reference knowledge base of Unix binaries that can be abused for privesc; often used to validate linPEAS-highlighted SUID/sudo vectors.

related_tools.name: Linux Smart Enumeration (lse)
related_tools.relationship: Alternative Linux enumeration script emphasizing verbosity levels and targeted selection; complementary to or substitutive for linPEAS in some workflows.

related_tools.name: Linux Exploit Suggester (and forks)
related_tools.relationship: Separate utilities that, given kernel/build info, suggest known kernel exploits; sometimes embedded or referenced by linPEAS variants for context.

evidence_gaps: Public documentation of every individual linPEAS check is spread across the HackTricks wiki and repository; a single authoritative, versioned specification of all checks is not centralized.
Behavioral guarantees (e.g., never writing to disk under all modes) can vary with options; definitive mode-by-mode side-effect documentation is limited.
Claims about specific, very recent check additions change frequently with releases; exact coverage at any given date should be confirmed against the current GitHub README and release notes.

sources.title: PEASS-ng repository (top-level README) – suite overview, releases, parsers, and linPEAS entry

PEASS-ng parsers – transforming PEASS outputs to JSON/HTML/PDF
HackTricks – Linux Privilege Escalation checklist and linPEAS references
GTFOBins project – curated Unix binaries abuse reference
Linux Smart Enumeration (lse) – alternative Linux privesc enumerator
